MINI MINUTES:

Hearing Re: PLAINTIFF RAYMOND DIGIACOMO's Motion To Amend Second Amended Complaint.

No appearances made in this matter. The Court adopts its tentative ruling.

The matter is continued for hearing to March 10, 2025. Motion And Notice Of Motion To Require Vexatious Litigant Raymond Digiacomo, Jr. To Furnish Security
Off Calendar Feb-03-2025 Text Ruling
MINI MINUTES:

Hearing Re: DEFENDANT VIGILANT EYE SECURITY, INC.'s Motion To Require Vexatious Litigant Raymond Digiacomo, Jr. To Furnish Security.

No appearances made in this matter. The Court adopts its tentative ruling.

Defendant's motion to require vexatious litigant DiGiacomo to furnish a security is granted.

Plaintiff DiGiacomo was declared a vexatious litigant on June 9, 2023. (Shryne Group Index of Exhibits, Ex. B.) Pursuant to CCP 391.1, defendant seeks to have plaintiff post a $15,000 security because "there is not a reasonable probability that [plaintiff] will prevail in the litigation against the moving defendant." (CCP 391.1(a).) The court must weigh the evidence to decide whether plaintiff has a reasonable chance of prevailing. (Golin v. Allenby (2010) 190 Cal.App.4th 616, 635.)

Plaintiff alleges that Defendant violated the Unruh Act based on his race. (Second Amended Complaint, par. 30.) Plaintiff complains that defendant denied him equal access based on his race because: (1) he was stopped and told he needed to comply with a sign which instructed patrons to put their purchase in plastic bag and (2) threatened that his name would be placed in a punitive database. (Id. at pars. 19-21.)

In Mackey v. Board of Trustees of California State University (2019) 31 Cal.App.5th 640, 660-661, the court explains that:

California's Unruh Act creates a cause of action for any person who is denied the right to "full and equal accommodations, advantages, facilities, privileges, or services in all business establishments of every kind whatsoever" based on that person's "sex, race, color, religion, ancestry, national origin, disability, medical condition, genetic information, marital status, sexual orientation, citizenship, primary language, or immigration status." [citation] The Unruh Act requires proof of intentional acts of race discrimination and does not cover disparate impact. [citations]

Here, Defendant's evidence shows that the above-cited conduct was not the result of intentional racial animus. Defendant's personnel never made any racial comments towards plaintiff. (Plaintiff Decl., Ex. 12 [Plaintiff's Depo., at 83:16-84:3; 124:1-127:13.) Defendant's reminder of the bag policy was a reasonable attempt to comply with the law. "All cannabis goods purchased by a customer shall not leave the licensed retailer's premises unless the goods are placed in an opaque exit package." (Cal. Code Regs. tit. 4, 15413(c).) Additionally, Plaintiff's Exhibit 53 demonstrates that notes were made regarding others who refused bags.

Defendant shows that Plaintiff does not have a reasonable probability of prevailing. The weight of the evidence shows that Plaintiff's claim is based on a conjectural subjective feeling of discriminatory animus rather than competent proof of invidious racial discrimination. (Plaintiff Decl., Ex. 12 [Plaintiff's Depo., at 83:16-84:3.) The motion is therefore granted.

Defendant's request for a $15,000 security is reasonable. Plaintiff shall furnish the security by March 3, 2025.

Counsel for the prevailing party is to prepare and submit a proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the Court's ruling with a cover letter outlining compliance with CRC 3.1312.

Judge: Christine Van Aken; Clerk: V. Da Fonseca; Not reported. (301/CVA)

Richard McKie, Esq. of Moser Legal P.C., Tel. 310.295.1724, appearing for the Defendants Shryne Group Inc., Shryne USA LLC, Stiiizy LLC, and Stiiizy Development LLC.

Hearing re: PLAINTIFF RAYMOND DIGIACOMO'S Motion For Order Modifying Protective Order Re DFP5 And Imposing $6,855 Monetary Sanction.

This matter is contested. Argument is held. At the conclusion of argument, the Court adopts its tentative ruling as follows:

The court adopts the Revised Report and Recommendation of JPT Cohen, which states:

In his moving papers, Plaintiff seeks a modification of the protective order to allow him to review the photographs of all of Defendant's customers in order to determine their race. This is an expensive proposition not warranted here. Code of Civil Procedure section 2031.060(f)(4) provides that the Court "shall limit the extent of discovery of electronically stored information, even from a source that is reasonably accessible, if the court determines that any of the following conditions exist: . . . (4) The likely burden or expense of the proposed discovery outweighs the likely benefit, taking into account the amount in controversy, the resources of the parties, the importance of the issues in the litigation, and the importance of the requested discovery in resolving the issues." Code Civ. Proc. 2031.060(f)(4) (emphasis added).

Vexatious litigant DiGiacomo's only argument in Dept 525 was that there should be a court reporter so that he could preserve issues for appeal. He has no right to a court reporter under the circumstances of this case and his motion should be denied.

Order signed.

MOTION TO STRIKE Amended COMPLAINT
Off Calendar Feb-09-2024 Text Ruling
MINI MINUTES:
No appearances.

There being no appearances, the tentative ruling on Defendant Vigilant Eye Security, Inc.'s Amended Motion to Strike Amended Complaint is adopted as follows: Defendant Vigilant Eye Security, Inc.'s motion to strike is denied. The court liberally construes the second amended complaint pursuant to Code of Civil Procedure ("CCP") section 452 and defendant fails to show that the cited allegations and exhibit are "irrelevant, false, or improper" within the meaning of CCP 436(a).

The court grants defendant's request for judicial notice since it may properly take judicial notice of the records of the State Bar of California. (Evidence Code section 452 (c); Rodas v. Spiegel (2001) 87 Cal.App.4th 513, 518 [judicial notice of official acts (including records) of legislative, executive, or judicial departments].)

Defendant fails to show that the court should strike the cited allegations/exhibit based on the litigation privilege of Civil Code section 47(b). Defendant erroneously relies on the "usual formulation" of the litigation privilege that applies to communications made during the course of a judicial proceeding. (Bergstein v. Stroock & Stroock & Lavan LLP (2015) 236 Cal.App.4th 793, 814 ["Under the usual formulation of the privilege, it applies to any communication (1) made in judicial or quasi-judicial proceedings; (2) by litigants or other participants authorized by law; (3) to achieve the objects of the litigation; and (4) that have some connection or logical relation to the action." (internal italics omitted)].) Defendant, however, seeks to strike pre-litigation communications and a different test applies. (Action Apartment Assn. v. City of Santa Monica (2007) 41 Cal.4th 1232, 1251 [To be privileged, a prelitigation communication "must be 'in furtherance of the objects of the litigation' " and "relate[ ] to litigation that is contemplated in good faith and under serious consideration."]; Cornell v. Berkeley Tennis Club (2017) 18 Cal.App.5th 908, 947 [same].)

A factual issue remains whether Mr. Lightman was contemplating litigation in good faith when he threatened to sue plaintiff. (Action Apartment, 41 Cal.4th at 1251 ["Whether a prelitigation communication relates to litigation that is contemplated in good faith and under serious consideration is an issue of fact."].) To the extent defendant argues that the allegations/exhibit are irrelevant, that argument founders. The cited information is germane background information and supports plaintiff's claim that plaintiff did not receive full and equal accommodations under the Unruh Act (Civil Code sec. 51). Order signed.

Judge: Rochelle C. East. Clerk: Jacqueline Alameda. Bailiff: Deputy Jaime. Not Reported. (302/RCE)

DEMURRER to Amended COMPLAINT
Off Calendar Apr-14-2023 Overruled
MINI MINUTES: DEFENDANT SHRYNE GROUP, INC., SHRYNE USA, LLC, STIIIZY, LLC DEMURRER to Amended COMPLAINT

No appearance.

The court adopted its tentative ruling on DEFENDANTS SHRYNE GROUP, INC., SHRYNE USA, LLC, STIIIZY, LLC'S DEMURRER to 1ST Amended COMPLAINT as follows:

Defendants' demurrer to the complaint is overruled as to the sole cause of action for violation of the Unruh Act. The Unruh Act "prohibits intentional discrimination in access to public accommodations." Brown v. Smith (1997) 55 Cal.App.4th 767, 786-787; see also Civil Code sec. 51 et seq. Defendants argue that plaintiff does not plead sufficient facts showing that he was discriminated against because of his race. See e.g., Reply at 2 (plaintiff fails to allege conduct was "substantially motivated by his race"). But as noted below, the amended complaint at para. 30 alleges exactly that.

In any event, the court must liberally construe the complaint under CCP sec. 452 and plaintiff sufficiently alleges that he was denied equal treatment because of his race. Among other things, plaintiff alleges that defendant (through its "minority" employees) engaged in an "upsale" business practice because plaintiff was "non-minority." (Amended Complaint, pars. 11-16; 30 ["A substantial MOTIVATING REASON for Defendants' conduct was their perception of Plaintiff's race." (emphasis in original)].) That conduct violates the Unruh Act, which mandates that business establishments "serve all persons without arbitrary discrimination." Angelucci v. Century Supper Club (2007) 41 Cal.4th 160, 167. Whether defendant in fact acted with a discriminatory animus raises a factual issue that cannot be resolved on demurrer.

Plaintiff's complaint also refers to other actions and inactions of defendant that allegedly support the claim. Defendant contends that these other actions and inactions are not actionable. But a party cannot demur to part of a cause of action. Elder v. Pacific Bell Telephone Co. (2012) 205 Cal.App.4th 841, 856 "[A] general demurrer does not lie as to a portion of a cause of action and if any part of a cause of action is properly pleaded, the demurrer will be overruled."]; Munoz v. Patel (2022) 81 Cal.App.5th 761, 780 n.9.

ORDER signed after hearing.

Judge: Curtis E.A. Karnow; Clerk: Gina Gonzales; Proceedings not reported. (302/CK)
